claim: Effective AI-assisted coding requires the human to act as architect—defining code structure, data flow, and state management—while the model handles implementation details; unguided code generation produces unmaintainable code
actionable: When working with coding agents, first sketch your mental model of architecture and data flow, then prompt agents with those constraints; avoid asking agents to design systems from scratch
source_title: Agentic Engineering in Action with Mitchell Hashimoto

claim: Detailed, constrained prompts with guardrails (like bowling bumpers) work best for agents; they're analogous to how you'd guide junior engineers—specific enough to prevent mistakes but open enough for initiative
actionable: Frame agent prompts as if briefing a junior engineer: specify the goal, the shape of the solution, key constraints, and expected data flow; avoid open-ended problem statements
source_title: Agentic Engineering in Action with Mitchell Hashimoto

claim: Always run generated code before reviewing it; don't assume broken code when you see unmaintainable code—first verify functionality, then refactor for quality
actionable: In your agentic workflow: (1) run the code to verify correctness, (2) review for maintainability, (3) refactor if needed; separate verification from code review phases
source_title: Agentic Engineering in Action with Mitchell Hashimoto

claim: Refactoring is one task LLMs excel at consistently; they reliably restructure code, rename variables, and consolidate logic with minimal errors—use agents here to save time
actionable: Delegate refactoring tasks to agents (extracting functions, consolidating logic, reorganizing conditionals) with high confidence; reserve manual editing for business logic and architecture changes
source_title: Agentic Engineering in Action with Mitchell Hashimoto

claim: LLMs struggle with high-performance data structure optimization; they don't understand memory locality, cache-friendliness, or why you'd use offset pointers instead of standard pointers—avoid agents for performance-sensitive code
actionable: For performance-critical code (tight loops, custom data structures, memory-layout optimization), don't use agents; reserve agent assistance for non-performance-sensitive refactors or business logic
source_title: Agentic Engineering in Action with Mitchell Hashimoto

claim: LLMs are bad at architectural decisions for large systems; they can't understand how to lay out subsystems or design APIs that will work across a large team—this still requires human judgment
actionable: For architectural decisions (subsystem boundaries, API design, module organization), rely on human architects; use agents only within architectures defined by humans
source_title: Agentic Engineering in Action with Mitchell Hashimoto

claim: Claude is more capable than competing LLMs (Gemini 2.5, others) for code generation; Gemini produces verbose responses and has lost ground in quality, while Claude remains most effective
actionable: For coding agents, prioritize Claude; test other models in parallel only when you have low confidence, not for routine work
source_title: Agentic Engineering in Action with Mitchell Hashimoto

claim: LLMs are competent at reading unfamiliar languages (like Zig) and understanding code logic, but not at modifying it; use them to explain Zig code or rewrite logic in languages they're better at, then you port back
actionable: For underrepresented languages, use agents to understand code logic and generate solutions in well-supported languages (Rust, Python, C), then manually port back to the target language
source_title: Agentic Engineering in Action with Mitchell Hashimoto

claim: LLMs are excellent at UI generation from screenshots; you can show a screenshot and ask it to reproduce the design in SwiftUI or React with >90% accuracy, eliminating tedious layout work
actionable: For UI building, provide reference screenshots to agents and ask them to reproduce the design; review and iterate on layouts rather than hand-coding them
source_title: Agentic Engineering in Action with Mitchell Hashimoto

claim: Using agents in parallel on the same task (four checkouts, four models) allows competitive testing; try multiple models and pick the best output without the context-switching overhead of sequential trials
actionable: For uncertain tasks, create multiple project checkouts and run agents in parallel with different models or prompting strategies; merge the best results instead of iterating sequentially
source_title: Agentic Engineering in Action with Mitchell Hashimoto

claim: LLMs often miss comments when refactoring code but will update outdated comments if you ask them; request comment review as part of code generation, treating it as a verification step
actionable: After agents modify code, explicitly ask them to review and update all related comments to match the new implementation; this catches both missed comments and outdated documentation
source_title: Agentic Engineering in Action with Mitchell Hashimoto

claim: Ask agents to review the last commit for improvements after you finish a change; they'll catch typos, inconsistent comments, broken cross-file references, and logic errors that automated tools miss
actionable: In your agentic workflow, always end with a prompt like 'review this commit and suggest improvements'; use the findings to fix documentation mismatches and cross-file consistency issues
source_title: Agentic Engineering in Action with Mitchell Hashimoto

claim: Don't expect agents to automate testing and verification for complex projects (especially GUI apps); disable self-testing in system prompts and rely on manual QA instead
actionable: For projects where agents can't automate testing (GUI apps, hardware integration), explicitly disable agent self-testing in system prompts and do manual verification yourself
source_title: Agentic Engineering in Action with Mitchell Hashimoto

claim: Treat agents like junior engineers: keep problems well-scoped, add guardrails, expect them to produce working solutions but not production-quality code; you'll be most effective if you don't expect them to think like seniors
actionable: Frame agent tasks as 'junior engineer problems' with clear scope and constraints; expect to do architectural thinking and senior-level code review yourself
source_title: Agentic Engineering in Action with Mitchell Hashimoto

claim: Build comprehensive comments and documentation now, even though LLMs can update them; the extra clarity helps agents make better decisions and creates a fail-safe check (comment-to-code mismatch signals a bug)
actionable: Maintain detailed comments (three lines per line of code for complex logic); use these as guardrails for agent prompts and as a verification mechanism (comment-code mismatch = bug signal)
source_title: Agentic Engineering in Action with Mitchell Hashimoto

claim: When refactoring large systems, keep both old and new implementations in the codebase until refactor is complete; LLMs generate better code when they can see the old patterns they're replacing
actionable: For large refactors, duplicate the affected module/folder (old and new), implement changes in parallel, then delete the old version at the end; agents will use old code as a reference pattern
source_title: Agentic Engineering in Action with Mitchell Hashimoto

claim: Tools need a long ramp-up period before they provide value; don't dismiss agents because your first attempts are slower—you'll eventually become more effective than the old workflow, just like learning Vim or version control
actionable: When adopting agents, expect an initial productivity dip; commit to learning your tools well before judging their value; track skill growth over weeks, not days
source_title: Agentic Engineering in Action with Mitchell Hashimoto

claim: Build your intuition for what agents can and can't do; don't use them for hopeless tasks repeatedly, and develop a sense of when to be specific vs. open-ended in prompts based on past experience
actionable: Keep a mental model of what your agent excels at and what's hopeless; periodically retry categorized tasks to catch improvements; this experience compounds over time
source_title: Agentic Engineering in Action with Mitchell Hashimoto

claim: While agents work, use the freed-up time to learn new things (new frameworks, new platforms) rather than switching tasks; this avoids context thrashing and compounds your skills in parallel
actionable: When an agent is working, block time for intentional learning (reading docs, watching talks, studying new languages) rather than task-switching; keep a learning queue
source_title: Agentic Engineering in Action with Mitchell Hashimoto
